questions.question: Relative to the browser paint, when does `useLayoutEffect` fire?
options: Synchronously after DOM mutation but before the browser paints
Asynchronously after the browser paints the new frame
Before React commits any DOM mutations
On the server during SSR, before hydration begins
questions.explanation: `useLayoutEffect` runs synchronously after React commits DOM mutations but before paint, letting you measure and adjust layout with no visible flicker. Firing after paint describes `useEffect`, and it does not run on the server.

questions.question: Which hook is the recommended default for data fetching, subscriptions, logging, and timers?
options: `useEffect`, because it runs asynchronously after paint and doesn't block rendering
`useLayoutEffect`, because blocking paint guarantees the data is ready
`useImperativeHandle`, because side effects belong on the ref
`useSyncExternalStore`, because all subscriptions must go through it
questions.explanation: `useEffect` is the default for non-layout side effects since it runs after paint and doesn't delay what the user sees. `useLayoutEffect` is reserved for layout reads that must happen before paint.

questions.question: What problem does `useId` primarily solve?
options: Producing a stable, unique ID that matches between server render and client hydration
Generating efficient, collision-free keys for items in a rendered list
Creating globally unique database primary keys on the client
Memoizing an expensive computed value across renders
questions.explanation: `useId` produces deterministic IDs so SSR markup and client markup agree and React doesn't warn about a hydration mismatch. It is explicitly NOT for list keys (those come from data identity).

questions.question: `useDebugValue` attaches a label to a custom hook that appears in React DevTools and has no runtime effect on the component's behavior.
questions.explanation: `useDebugValue` is purely a debugging aid surfaced in DevTools; it does not change component behavior at runtime.

questions.question: What is the main reason to use `useImperativeHandle`?
options: Expose a narrow, intentional imperative API on a ref while keeping the underlying DOM node private
Replace props and state as the primary mechanism for passing data to children
Subscribe a component to an external store without tearing
Generate hydration-safe IDs for form controls
questions.explanation: It customizes the handle exposed through a ref so a parent can call a deliberate API (`focus()`, `play()`) without reaching into arbitrary internals. Data flow should still use props/state, not this escape hatch.

questions.question: In `useSyncExternalStore`, why must `getSnapshot()` return a referentially stable value when the underlying data hasn't changed?
options: Returning a fresh object each call makes React think the value changed every render, causing an infinite re-render loop
React serializes the snapshot to JSON, and unstable values fail serialization
A new reference each call disables concurrent rendering for the whole tree
Unstable snapshots are silently ignored, so subscriptions never fire
questions.explanation: React compares snapshot references to decide whether to re-render; building a new object/array every call always looks like a change and loops infinitely. Caching and returning the same reference for unchanged data prevents this.

questions.question: What concurrency hazard does `useSyncExternalStore` specifically prevent that a naive `useState` + `useEffect` subscription does not?
options: Tearing — different parts of the UI reading different values because the store changed mid-render
Memory leaks from forgetting to unsubscribe on unmount
Hydration mismatches caused by non-deterministic IDs
Layout flicker from measuring the DOM after paint
questions.explanation: In concurrent rendering, an effect-based subscription can read a value that changed mid-render, so the UI tears. `useSyncExternalStore` guarantees a consistent snapshot for the whole render, avoiding tearing.

questions.question: A component needs several related IDs (a field, its hint, its error message). What is recommended?
options: Call `useId` once and append suffixes like `${id}-hint` and `${id}-error`
Call `useId` separately for each element to guarantee independence
Use the array index of each element as its ID
Generate the IDs with `Math.random()` so they never collide
questions.explanation: Calling `useId` once and suffixing keeps related IDs consistent and unique with a single hook call. Multiple `useId` calls are unnecessary, and random IDs break hydration determinism.

questions.question: Using `useLayoutEffect` in server-rendered code logs a warning because there is no layout to measure before hydration.
questions.explanation: `useLayoutEffect` does not run on the server; in server-rendered code it warns. The fix is to guard it or move the layout read into `useEffect`.

questions.question: What is the purpose of passing a format function as the second argument to `useDebugValue`, e.g. `useDebugValue(date, d => d.toISOString())`?
options: React calls the formatter only when DevTools inspects the value, keeping expensive formatting off the hot path
It runs the formatter on every render to keep the label up to date
It memoizes the value so the hook skips re-running when the input is unchanged
It validates the value's type and throws if formatting fails
questions.explanation: The optional formatter is lazy: React invokes it only when DevTools actually inspects the hook, so costly formatting doesn't run on normal renders.

questions.question: How does `ref` work with function components in React 19 when using `useImperativeHandle`?
options: `ref` is a normal prop, so no `forwardRef` wrapper is needed (though `forwardRef` still works)
`forwardRef` is now mandatory and the only supported way to receive a ref
Function components cannot receive refs; you must convert to a class
`ref` must be passed through React Context instead of as a prop
questions.explanation: In React 19 `ref` is just a regular prop, so a function component can accept it directly without `forwardRef`. `forwardRef` still works but is slated for deprecation.

questions.question: What happens to the exposed handle if you omit the dependency array in `useImperativeHandle(ref, factory)`?
options: The handle is recreated on every render
The handle is created once and frozen for the component's lifetime
React throws because `deps` is required
The handle is recreated only when the ref identity changes
questions.explanation: Like other hooks, omitting `deps` causes the factory to run and recreate the handle every render. Supplying `deps` recreates it only when a dependency changes.

questions.question: Given this tooltip code, why is `useLayoutEffect` (not `useEffect`) the correct choice?
options: It measures and repositions before paint, so the tooltip never flashes at the wrong position
`useEffect` cannot call `getBoundingClientRect`, which only works synchronously
`useLayoutEffect` runs during server rendering, ensuring the position is correct in the SSR HTML
`useEffect` would never re-run when `targetRef` changes, so the position would be stale
questions.explanation: Reading layout and setting state in `useLayoutEffect` happens before paint, so the user never sees the tooltip in the wrong spot. With `useEffect` the initial (top:0) frame would paint first, causing a visible jump.

questions.question: `useId` is an appropriate way to generate the `key` for each item when rendering a list with `.map()`.
questions.explanation: `useId` is per-component-instance, not per-list-item, so it's the wrong tool for keys. Keys must come from your data's stable identity.

questions.question: In this `useSyncExternalStore` call, what is the role of the third argument `() => true`?
options: It is `getServerSnapshot`, supplying the value used during SSR and initial hydration when `navigator` is unavailable
It is a fallback subscribe function used if the first one throws
It forces the component to always re-render on every store change
It is the unsubscribe function returned to React
questions.explanation: The optional third argument is `getServerSnapshot`, used during server rendering and initial hydration (here assuming online), since `navigator.onLine` isn't available on the server.

questions.question: Which scenarios are legitimate use cases for `useLayoutEffect` over `useEffect`? (Select all that apply.)
options: Measuring an element's size to position a popover before the user sees the first frame
Auto-scrolling a chat container to the bottom without a visible jump
Fetching JSON from an API when the component mounts
Logging analytics events after the component renders
Reading `getBoundingClientRect` to synchronously adjust the DOM before paint
questions.explanation: `useLayoutEffect` is for layout reads/adjustments that must occur before paint (popover positioning, flicker-free auto-scroll, `getBoundingClientRect` adjustments). Fetching and analytics logging are non-layout work best left in `useEffect`.

questions.question: Which statements about `useSyncExternalStore` are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `subscribe` must return an unsubscribe function that React calls on cleanup
It is mainly library plumbing; app code usually consumes a wrapper like `useSelector`
`getSnapshot` should return a new object literal on every call to ensure freshness
It exists to keep snapshots consistent and avoid tearing under concurrent rendering
It is primarily intended for generating accessibility IDs
questions.explanation: `subscribe` returns the unsubscribe function, the hook is library plumbing wrapped by `useSelector`/Zustand hooks, and its purpose is tearing-free consistent snapshots. Returning a fresh object each call causes infinite loops, and ID generation is `useId`'s job.

questions.question: Why does React in development StrictMode mount an effect, run its cleanup, then mount it again?
options: To surface missing or incorrect cleanup, pushing you to write idempotent setup with a matching teardown
To benchmark whether `useLayoutEffect` or `useEffect` is faster for the effect
Because effects always run twice in production for reliability
To pre-fetch data twice so the cache is warm before paint
questions.explanation: StrictMode's dev-only mount/cleanup/re-mount cycle exposes effects that lack proper cleanup, encouraging idempotent setup paired with a correct teardown. This behavior is development-only, not production.
